petitioner_background: The petitioner is a master mariner (ship captain qualified to command vessels of any size) and specialist surveyor who inspects vessels, provides technical advice, and offers arbitration and expert testimony. He holds a Law degree and is certified as a casualty investigator and Lead Auditor, authorizing him to conduct ship verifications and certification. He sought classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under EB-1A to run a marine surveying and consulting company in the United States. He also served as president of a fishing company that he helped recover from financial crisis.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner initially claimed several awards but abandoned most of these claims on appeal, discussing only one: an 'Honorary Helmsman Award for his dedicated efforts towards academic development, forming future generations of seafarers,' presented to him by a university.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director determined that this award appears to be limited to the staff and faculty of one university, and that the petitioner had not established the national or international recognition of the award. However, the AAO did not explore these questions because of a more fundamental issue: the petitioner must meet all eligibility requirements at the time of filing under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1). When he filed the petition in October 2018, he did not mention the Honorary Helmsman Award, and subsequent submissions show he did not receive the award until December 2018, nearly two months after filing. Therefore, the award cannot show eligibility at the time of filing.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: After initially claiming various memberships, the petitioner limited his arguments on appeal to his full membership in the International Institute of Marine Surveying (IIMS). He submitted a printout from the IIMS website stating that 'IIMS membership is open to professional marine surveyors...who have sufficient and relevant experience and who meet the Institute's joining criteria.' Full membership is available to: (1) anyone who can prove they have practiced on a full-time basis for a minimum of five years as a consultant or marine surveyor; (2) individuals who, by producing written reports, can demonstrate they have practiced marine surveying or consultancy for at least five years; (3) individuals whose qualifications or experience shall be considered appropriate by the Professional Assessment Committee.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The IIMS's own website does not describe the restrictive membership requirements that the regulation demands. Five years' experience is not an outstanding achievement, and the petitioner has not shown that the Professional Assessment Committee consists of recognized national or international experts.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The record contains five articles from 2015-16 about efforts to revitalize a fishing company. The articles identify the petitioner as the president of the company and many of them quote him talking about the company.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The articles focus on the company rather than on the petitioner and his work there. Also, the petitioner claims extraordinary ability as a master mariner and specialist surveyor, and while these fields have some relevance to the fishing industry, the articles do not appear to relate to his work as a master mariner or specialist surveyor. The 13-page introductory letter accompanying the petition discussed his 'deep experience at sea as a deck officer,' his accreditation as a 'Surveyor and Auditor,' his educational work as a college lecturer, and his work as a 'casualty investigator,' but contained no mention of the fishing company, and the articles about the fishing company do not discuss any of those listed facets of his career. As such, the submitted articles are, at best, tangential to the areas in which the petitioner specifically claims extraordinary ability.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Initially, the petitioner claimed two specific contributions relating to maritime law and teaching courses, but did not pursue these claims on appeal. In response to a request for evidence, the petitioner stated that he 'was appointed as the president of [the fishing company] by an official resolution' because he was 'the most qualified person to handle the financial crisis of the fishing company.' He asserted that he 'was able to recover the company, paid the debts of all the employees, recover[ed] vessels and paid outstanding debts with creditors.' On appeal, he discussed the economic importance of the fishing company as a major employer and food source.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director stated that the petitioner did not establish how his work for the fishing company relates to the field of marine surveying and consulting, which is the area in which he claims extraordinary ability. On appeal, the petitioner discussed the economic importance of the fishing company but did not address this fundamental point. Because the petitioner has not shown that his work for the fishing company was a contribution in the field of maritime surveying and consulting, discussion of the significance of the contribution cannot establish eligibility.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner initially claimed to meet this criterion but did not contest the Director's conclusions on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The petitioner does not contest the Director's conclusions regarding criterion (vi), and therefore the AAO considered that issue to be abandoned, citing Matter of R-A-M-, 25 I&N Dec. 657.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of a leading or critical role for distinguished organizations or establishments.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The Director concluded that the petitioner met this criterion, and the AAO stated it would not disturb that conclusion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed high remuneration for services.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO reserved this issue, stating that detailed discussion of this remaining criterion cannot change the outcome of the appeal because the petitioner has only satisfied one of the criteria discussed. The AAO cited INS v. Bagamasbad, 429 U.S. 24, and Matter of L-A-C-, 26 I&N Dec. 516, for the proposition that agencies are not required to make findings unnecessary to the results they reach.

outcome_reasoning: The petitioner has not submitted the required initial evidence of either a one-time achievement or documents that meet at least three of the ten criteria, having satisfied only one criterion (leading or critical role). As a result, the AAO need not provide a final merits determination under Kazarian. Nevertheless, the AAO reviewed the record in the aggregate and concluded it does not support that the petitioner has established the required acclaim and recognition. The petitioner sought to establish eligibility with piecemeal evidence pertaining to somewhat related but separate and distinct fields (maritime surveying and executive leadership of a fishery) within the much broader category of maritime endeavors. The petitioner's assertion that his 'area of expertise is the maritime field' describes a broad umbrella term rather than a single, coherent field of endeavor, and he has not shown that his work is consistent with a 'career of acclaimed work in the field' or that he is among the small percentage at the very top of the field.
legal_citations: Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)
Sepulveda v. U.S. Att'y Gen., 401 F.3d 1226 (11th Cir. 2005)
United States v. Cunningham, 161 F.3d 1343 (11th Cir. 1998)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural history: The Director denied the petition, and the petitioner filed two combined motions to reopen and reconsider. On both occasions, the Director granted the motions, withdrew the prior decision, and issued a new denial notice. Because the Director withdrew the first two denial decisions, only the most recent decision was before the AAO on appeal. Additionally, the petitioner's claimed Honorary Helmsman Award was received in December 2018, nearly two months after the petition was filed in October 2018, meaning it could not establish eligibility at the time of filing under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(1). The petitioner also abandoned his claim under criterion (vi) for scholarly articles by not contesting it on appeal.
